question: A patient reports sudden inability to perceive the right half of the visual scene in both eyes after a stroke. Which descriptive label best matches this bilateral same-sided field loss?
cited_learning_objective: Recall, recognize, and apply the meanings of common descriptive terminology: hemianopia, quadrantanopia, homonymous, heteronymous, macular sparing

options.text: Right homonymous hemianopic field defect
options.rationale: Correct because loss of the same side of the visual field in both eyes is a homonymous hemianopia; the lecture states retrochiasmal lesions produce homonymous hemianopia.

options.text: Bitemporal hemianopic field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because bitemporal deficits affect the temporal halves of both visual fields; this would require a midline chiasmal lesion rather than a retrochiasmal stroke.

options.text: Left homonymous hemianopic field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because this labels the opposite side; changing the side of visual loss to left would make it correct for left-sided field loss instead.

options.text: Monocular central scotoma field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because a monocular central scotoma affects one eye only and central vision; bilateral same-sided half-field loss implies a postchiasmal lesion, not a monocular retinal or optic nerve lesion.

options.text: Right heteronymous quadrantic field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because heteronymous deficits involve different sides in each eye; a same-sided bilateral half-field loss is homonymous, not heteronymous.

question: During a bedside exam a student notes loss of peripheral temporal vision in both eyes in a patient with a slowly enlarging sellar mass. Which descriptive term best fits that pattern?
cited_learning_objective: Recall, recognize, and apply the meanings of common descriptive terminology: hemianopia, quadrantanopia, homonymous, heteronymous, macular sparing

options.text: Bitemporal hemianopic field defect
options.rationale: Correct because midline chiasmal compression damages crossing nasal retinal fibers producing loss of temporal visual fields in both eyes, as described for pituitary-region masses in the lecture.

options.text: Binasal hemianopic field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because binasal loss requires bilateral lateral chiasmal or bilateral optic nerve pathology; a midline sellar mass typically causes bitemporal loss instead.

options.text: Right homonymous hemianopic field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because homonymous defects affect the same side in both eyes due to retrochiasmal lesions; chiasmal compression produces heteronymous temporal loss.

options.text: Monocular peripheral field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because monocular peripheral loss affects one eye only; bilateral temporal loss implicates the chiasm rather than a single optic nerve or retina.

options.text: Concentric tunnel vision field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because concentric constriction implies diffuse retinal or optic nerve disease; focal chiasmal compression produces bitemporal hemianopia rather than uniform constriction.

question: A patient has a wedge-shaped loss of the upper-right quadrant of vision in both eyes after a temporal lobe contusion. Which localization best explains this pattern?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Left temporal optic radiation lesion
options.rationale: Correct because Meyer's (temporal) loop carries superior visual field fibers; a left temporal radiation lesion produces a right superior quadrantanopia, matching the wedge-shaped upper-right loss described in the lecture.

options.text: Left parietal optic radiation lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because parietal (superior) radiations carry inferior visual field fibers; a left parietal lesion would cause a right inferior quadrantanopia instead.

options.text: Right temporal optic radiation lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because a right temporal radiation lesion would affect left superior visual field; reversing laterality would make this option correct for left-sided quadrant loss.

options.text: Left lateral geniculate nucleus lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because LGN lesions typically cause contralateral homonymous hemianopia or sectoranopia but are less likely to produce an isolated superior quadrant wedge as cleanly as a temporal radiation lesion.

options.text: Right primary visual cortex lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because a right V1 lesion would cause left visual field deficits; the described right superior quadrant loss localizes to the left hemisphere, not right.

question: A patient reports difficulty locating objects in the lower-left visual quadrant after a parietal lobe infarct. Which concise descriptor matches that deficit?
cited_learning_objective: Recall, recognize, and apply the meanings of common descriptive terminology: hemianopia, quadrantanopia, homonymous, heteronymous, macular sparing

options.text: Right inferior quadrantanopic field defect
options.rationale: Correct because a left parietal (superior) optic radiation lesion produces a contralateral inferior quadrantanopia; the lecture notes parietal loop lesions cause opposite lower quadrant loss.

options.text: Left superior quadrantanopic field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because this names the opposite quadrant; changing laterality and vertical position would be required to match the described lower-left deficit.

options.text: Left homonymous hemianopic field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because a hemianopia involves half the field, not a single quadrant; a parietal radiation lesion typically yields a quadrant, not a full hemifield, unless larger.

options.text: Bitemporal hemianopic field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because bitemporal loss affects temporal fields of both eyes from chiasmal lesions; a parietal infarct produces a homonymous quadrant deficit instead.

options.text: Monocular inferior field defect
options.rationale: Wrong because monocular defects affect one eye only; bilateral quadrant loss in both eyes indicates a retrochiasmal pathway lesion.

question: An MRI shows a lesion confined to the left primary visual cortex. A patient can still read fine but reports missing the right peripheral field except the central area. Which term best describes this pattern?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Right homonymous hemianopic field defect with macular sparing
options.rationale: Correct because V1 lesions often spare macular representation due to dual arterial supply and large cortical representation, producing contralateral hemianopia with preserved central vision as described in the lecture.

options.text: Right homonymous hemianopic field defect without sparing
options.rationale: Wrong because absence of macular sparing would imply complete cortical involvement including macular cortex; the MRI finding of confined lesion and preserved central vision supports macular sparing instead.

options.text: Left homonymous hemianopic field defect with macular sparing
options.rationale: Wrong because laterality is reversed; a left V1 lesion causes right visual field loss, not left.

options.text: Bitemporal hemianopic field defect with macular sparing
options.rationale: Wrong because bitemporal loss arises from chiasmal damage and affects peripheral temporal fields, not a unilateral cortical lesion producing contralateral homonymous loss.

options.text: Monocular central scotoma with macular sparing
options.rationale: Wrong because monocular central scotoma affects one eye's central vision; macular sparing refers to cortical lesions and contralateral field preservation, not monocular retinal defects.

question: A patient with a lesion in the optic chiasm complains of gradual peripheral vision narrowing. Which mechanism from the lecture best explains the typical pattern produced by a midline chiasmal mass?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the following visual field deficits from history and presentation described in a clinical vignette, localize the lesioned visual system structure(s), and recognize likely etiologies: bitemporal hemianopia, nasal hemianopia, homonymous hemianopia, sectoranopia, quadrantanopia, homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing

options.text: Compression of crossing nasal retinal fibers
options.rationale: Correct because nasal retinal ganglion axons decussate at the chiasm and carry temporal visual field information; midline compression damages these crossing fibers producing bitemporal peripheral loss as the lecture explains.

options.text: Ischemia of ipsilateral optic tract fibers
options.rationale: Wrong because optic tract ischemia causes contralateral homonymous hemianopia rather than heteronymous temporal field loss from chiasmal compression.

options.text: Lesion of macular cortical representation
options.rationale: Wrong because macular cortical damage causes central visual loss or loss without sparing; chiasmal masses classically spare central macular fibers until late stages.

options.text: Damage to temporal retinal ganglion cells
options.rationale: Wrong because temporal retinal fibers remain ipsilateral and represent nasal visual fields; midline chiasmal compression primarily affects nasal retinal fibers that cross, not temporal retinal fibers.

options.text: Degeneration of optic nerve head axons
options.rationale: Wrong because optic nerve head degeneration produces monocular field defects or scotomas, not the bilateral temporal field constriction seen with chiasmal compression.

question: A patient with sudden right-sided weakness also reports inability to see the left half of the world. Which lesion location from the lecture most likely explains both contralateral motor and visual field deficits?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Left posterior cerebral artery territory infarct
options.rationale: Correct because PCA infarcts can involve occipital cortex producing contralateral homonymous hemianopia and may extend to adjacent structures causing motor findings; the lecture links PCA supply to visual cortex and macular sparing phenomena.

options.text: Left middle cerebral artery cortical infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because MCA infarcts more commonly produce motor and sensory deficits and may cause visual field cuts via optic radiations but PCA is the classic supply to primary visual cortex; if MCA involvement included temporal/parietal radiations it could cause quadrant defects rather than full hemianopia.

options.text: Right posterior cerebral artery territory infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because laterality is reversed; a right PCA infarct would produce left visual field loss, not the right-sided motor and left visual loss combination described for a left-sided lesion.

options.text: Midline optic chiasm compression
options.rationale: Wrong because chiasmal lesions produce heteronymous temporal field loss without contralateral motor deficits; they do not explain ipsilateral motor weakness.

options.text: Left optic tract hemorrhage
options.rationale: Wrong because isolated optic tract lesions cause contralateral homonymous hemianopia but do not typically produce motor weakness; involvement of motor pathways would require more extensive vascular territory involvement such as PCA cortical infarct.

question: A patient has a small infarct affecting the left lateral geniculate nucleus and presents with a contralateral wedge-shaped visual loss. Which lecture concept explains why LGN lesions can produce variable sector defects?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Variable LGN vascular supply causing sectoranopia
options.rationale: Correct because the LGN receives blood from multiple small arteries (anterior choroidal, thalamogeniculate, posterior choroidal), so infarcts can produce wedge or sector-shaped homonymous deficits as noted in the lecture.

options.text: Uniform LGN layer damage producing full hemianopia
options.rationale: Wrong because uniform damage across all LGN layers would cause a complete contralateral hemianopia; the lecture emphasizes that LGN infarcts are variable due to vascular supply, producing sectoranopias rather than uniform loss.

options.text: Compression of optic chiasm causing heteronymous loss
options.rationale: Wrong because chiasmal compression yields heteronymous temporal field loss, not LGN sector defects; the LGN is postchiasmal and produces homonymous deficits.

options.text: Meyer's loop injury producing superior quadrant loss
options.rationale: Wrong because Meyer's loop lesions in temporal lobe cause superior quadrantanopia; LGN vascular variability, not temporal loop injury, explains sectoranopia from LGN lesions.

options.text: Retinal ganglion cell degeneration causing monocular scotoma
options.rationale: Wrong because retinal ganglion degeneration causes monocular scotomas; LGN lesions produce contralateral homonymous defects due to thalamic involvement, not monocular retinal findings.

question: A patient with a lesion that spares the calcarine cortex representation of the macula has a contralateral visual field loss but preserved central vision. Which vascular explanation from the lecture accounts for this macular sparing?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Dual arterial supply to macular cortex producing sparing
options.rationale: Correct because the macular representation in V1 has overlapping supply from PCA and MCA branches, so cortical injuries often spare central vision as described in the lecture's macular sparing concept.

options.text: Redundant retinal arterial supply preserving macula
options.rationale: Wrong because retinal arterial redundancy would protect retinal tissue, not cortical macular representation; macular sparing is a cortical vascular phenomenon involving PCA/MCA anastomoses.

options.text: Collateral flow in optic radiations preserving central fibers
options.rationale: Wrong because optic radiation collaterals do not explain selective macular sparing at the cortical level; the lecture attributes macular sparing to cortical arterial anastomoses rather than radiation collaterals.

options.text: Selective LGN layer preservation of macular input
options.rationale: Wrong because LGN layer preservation would not typically produce the classic cortical macular sparing pattern; the lecture emphasizes cortical vascular supply as the mechanism.

options.text: Temporal lobe loop redundancy protecting central vision
options.rationale: Wrong because temporal (Meyer's) loop carries superior field fibers and does not selectively protect macular representation; macular sparing is cortical and vascular in origin.

question: A patient reports formed visual hallucinations of people after losing part of visual input from one eye. Which lecture phenomenon best explains these release-type perceptions?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Release phenomena from visual deprivation
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes Charles Bonnet–type release hallucinations occurring when visual deprivation leads to formed visual hallucinations due to disinhibition in visual association cortices.

options.text: Occipital seizure producing simple phosphenes
options.rationale: Wrong because occipital seizures typically cause simple flashing lights or geometric shapes; formed complex hallucinations from deprivation are release phenomena in association cortex.

options.text: Toxic metabolic encephalopathy causing formed visions
options.rationale: Wrong because while metabolic disturbances can cause hallucinations, the lecture specifically links formed visual hallucinations after visual loss to release phenomena rather than generalized metabolic causes.

options.text: Primary visual cortex hyperexcitability causing faces
options.rationale: Wrong because primary visual cortex lesions usually cause elementary visual disturbances; complex formed images implicate inferior visual association cortex or release phenomena, not V1 hyperexcitability alone.

options.text: Auditory cortex cross-activation producing visual images
options.rationale: Wrong because cross-activation of auditory cortex would not produce visual hallucinations; the lecture attributes formed visual hallucinations to visual association areas and release phenomena after deprivation.

question: A patient with a lesion in the right optic radiation near the posterior limb of the internal capsule reports loss of the left lower visual quadrant. Which pathway detail from the lecture explains why a parietal loop lesion produces this specific quadrant loss?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Parietal loop carries inferior visual field fibers
options.rationale: Correct because the parietal (superior) optic radiation transmits inferior visual field information to the superior bank of the calcarine sulcus, so a right parietal lesion causes left inferior quadrantanopia as the lecture describes.

options.text: Temporal loop carries inferior visual field fibers
options.rationale: Wrong because the temporal (Meyer's) loop carries superior visual field fibers; confusing loops would invert the expected quadrant and make this option incorrect.

options.text: LGN magnocellular layers carry superior field only
options.rationale: Wrong because magnocellular layers convey motion and broad spatial info, not exclusively superior field; quadrant localization depends on radiation path, not solely LGN layer identity.

options.text: Optic chiasm decussation causes quadrant loss
options.rationale: Wrong because chiasmal decussation produces heteronymous temporal field defects, not homonymous quadrant losses from parietal radiation lesions.

options.text: Macular representation lies in temporal lobe
options.rationale: Wrong because macular representation is in the occipital pole of V1, not the temporal lobe; temporal lobe involvement affects superior field fibers via Meyer's loop, not macular mapping.

question: A patient with a slowly enlarging suprasellar tumor develops progressive peripheral vision loss and endocrine symptoms. Which diagnosis from the lecture best fits this combination?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the following visual field deficits from history and presentation described in a clinical vignette, localize the lesioned visual system structure(s), and recognize likely etiologies: bitemporal hemianopia, nasal hemianopia, homonymous hemianopia, sectoranopia, quadrantanopia, homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing

options.text: Pituitary adenoma compressing optic chiasm
options.rationale: Correct because pituitary-region tumors commonly compress the midline optic chiasm producing bitemporal peripheral field loss and often cause endocrine disturbances, as noted in the lecture's chiasmal lesion etiologies.

options.text: Meningioma of parietal convexity
options.rationale: Wrong because parietal meningiomas typically produce contralateral quadrant or hemifield deficits depending on radiation involvement, not the classic bitemporal loss with endocrine signs from a sellar mass.

options.text: Craniopharyngioma in temporal lobe
options.rationale: Wrong because craniopharyngiomas are sellar/suprasellar and can cause chiasmal compression; specifying temporal lobe location makes this option anatomically inconsistent—if placed suprasellarly it could be correct.

options.text: Internal carotid artery aneurysm compressing optic tract
options.rationale: Wrong because ICA aneurysms can compress lateral chiasm or optic nerve causing nasal field defects or monocular signs; they less commonly produce the midline bitemporal pattern with endocrine symptoms typical of pituitary adenoma.

options.text: Posterior cerebral artery aneurysm causing occipital infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because PCA aneurysm or infarct affects occipital cortex producing homonymous field loss and visual cortex signs, not the progressive bitemporal peripheral loss with pituitary-related endocrine features.

question: A patient is found to have loss of the nasal visual field in the right eye only. Which lesion location from the lecture most plausibly produces an ipsilateral nasal field defect?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the following visual field deficits from history and presentation described in a clinical vignette, localize the lesioned visual system structure(s), and recognize likely etiologies: bitemporal hemianopia, nasal hemianopia, homonymous hemianopia, sectoranopia, quadrantanopia, homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing

options.text: Right lateral optic chiasm lesion
options.rationale: Correct because lateral chiasmal damage affects ipsilateral temporal retinal fibers that represent the nasal visual field, producing an ipsilateral nasal hemianopia as described in the lecture.

options.text: Right optic tract lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because optic tract lesions produce contralateral homonymous hemianopia, not an isolated ipsilateral nasal field defect.

options.text: Left optic nerve lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because optic nerve lesions cause monocular deficits in the ipsilateral eye; a left optic nerve lesion would not produce a right-eye nasal field loss.

options.text: Left temporal optic radiation lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because temporal radiation lesions cause contralateral superior quadrant loss, not an isolated ipsilateral nasal field defect from lateral chiasmal compression.

options.text: Right lateral geniculate nucleus infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because LGN lesions cause contralateral homonymous defects or sectoranopias; an isolated ipsilateral nasal field loss points to lateral chiasmal involvement instead.

question: A patient with a lesion that interrupts fibers from the nasal retina of both eyes would present with which visual field pattern, and which common tumor type is classically responsible according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the following visual field deficits from history and presentation described in a clinical vignette, localize the lesioned visual system structure(s), and recognize likely etiologies: bitemporal hemianopia, nasal hemianopia, homonymous hemianopia, sectoranopia, quadrantanopia, homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing

options.text: Bitemporal hemianopic field defect from pituitary adenoma
options.rationale: Correct because damage to crossing nasal retinal fibers at the midline chiasm produces bitemporal field loss, and pituitary adenomas are a classic slow-growing cause of midline chiasmal compression as the lecture notes.

options.text: Binasal hemianopic field defect from meningioma
options.rationale: Wrong because binasal loss requires bilateral lateral compression and is less common; meningiomas can cause chiasmal effects but midline pituitary adenomas classically produce bitemporal loss.

options.text: Right homonymous hemianopic field defect from craniopharyngioma
options.rationale: Wrong because homonymous hemianopia indicates retrochiasmal involvement; craniopharyngiomas are sellar/suprasellar and more likely to cause bitemporal loss if compressing the chiasm.

options.text: Monocular scotoma from optic neuritis
options.rationale: Wrong because optic neuritis causes monocular central or diffuse defects, not bilateral temporal field loss from crossing fiber interruption at the chiasm.

options.text: Concentric visual constriction from glaucoma
options.rationale: Wrong because glaucoma produces peripheral constriction rather than the heteronymous bitemporal hemianopia resulting from chiasmal compression by pituitary tumors.

question: A patient with a lesion in the left inferior optic radiation reports loss of the right upper visual quadrant. Which two-step reasoning from the lecture leads to this localization?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Meyer's loop carries superior field fibers to left V1
options.rationale: Correct because Meyer's (temporal) loop transmits superior visual field information from the contralateral field to the inferior bank of V1; a left temporal radiation lesion therefore causes a right superior quadrantanopia, matching the lecture's parcellation of optic radiations.

options.text: Parietal loop carries superior field fibers to left V1
options.rationale: Wrong because the parietal loop carries inferior visual field fibers to the superior bank of V1; confusing loop functions would invert expected quadrant loss.

options.text: Left optic tract carries right upper field fibers
options.rationale: Wrong because the optic tract carries full contralateral visual field information, not isolated upper quadrant fibers; quadrant losses localize to radiations or cortex rather than the tract.

options.text: Left LGN exclusively carries superior field fibers
options.rationale: Wrong because LGN layers maintain retinotopy but do not exclusively segregate superior field fibers in a way that explains isolated superior quadrant loss; radiation path explains quadrant specificity.

options.text: Right temporal radiation lesion causing right upper loss
options.rationale: Wrong because laterality is incorrect; a right temporal lesion would affect the left visual field, not the right, so this option misassigns hemisphere and would be correct only if laterality were reversed.

question: A patient with a lesion limited to the right primary visual cortex reports a dense left visual hemifield loss but preserved central vision. Which two lecture facts combine to explain this finding?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Contralateral V1 lesion with macular cortical sparing
options.rationale: Correct because V1 lesions produce contralateral homonymous hemianopia and the macula is disproportionately represented and has dual arterial supply, often resulting in macular sparing as described in the lecture.

options.text: Contralateral optic tract lesion with macular sparing
options.rationale: Wrong because optic tract lesions cause homonymous hemianopia but macular sparing is a cortical vascular phenomenon tied to V1, not optic tract lesions.

options.text: Bitemporal chiasmal lesion with macular sparing
options.rationale: Wrong because chiasmal lesions produce heteronymous temporal field loss, not contralateral hemianopia; macular sparing is specific to cortical lesions.

options.text: Left V1 lesion with macular sparing
options.rationale: Wrong because laterality is reversed; a left V1 lesion would produce right visual field loss, not the left-sided deficit described.

options.text: LGN infarct producing central vision preservation
options.rationale: Wrong because LGN infarcts typically do not produce the classic macular sparing pattern tied to cortical arterial anastomoses; macular sparing implicates cortical V1 supply.

question: A patient with a right occipital lobe hemorrhage can still identify faces but cannot localize moving objects in the left visual field. Which lecture pathway distinction explains preserved object identity but impaired spatial localization?
cited_learning_objective: The primary visual cortex dissects incoming visual information to its component elements and distributes them to association areas for processing

options.text: Ventral stream intact for 'what' and dorsal stream impaired for 'where'
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes the ventral pathway to inferior temporal cortex mediating object recognition and the dorsal pathway to parietal cortex mediating spatial localization; occipital damage affecting dorsal projections impairs 'where' while sparing 'what'.

options.text: Dorsal stream intact for 'what' and ventral stream impaired for 'where'
options.rationale: Wrong because this reverses the functional assignments; the ventral stream conveys 'what' and the dorsal stream conveys 'where' as the lecture specifies.

options.text: Primary visual cortex solely mediates face recognition
options.rationale: Wrong because V1 processes basic features and distributes to association areas; face recognition depends on ventral temporal regions beyond V1, not V1 alone.

options.text: Superior colliculus damage causing face agnosia
options.rationale: Wrong because superior colliculus mediates orienting and reflexive visual responses, not complex face recognition; face agnosia implicates ventral temporal cortex.

options.text: LGN parvocellular loss causing motion localization deficit
options.rationale: Wrong because magnocellular LGN layers, not parvocellular, contribute to motion and spatial processing; the lecture links dorsal pathway and magnocellular input to 'where' functions.

question: A patient with progressive headaches and a sellar mass keeps bumping into objects on the left side and confrontation testing shows loss of the left inferior visual fields in both eyes. Using lecture logic, how should this visual deficit be described and where does it localize?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the following visual field deficits from history and presentation described in a clinical vignette, localize the lesioned visual system structure(s), and recognize likely etiologies: bitemporal hemianopia, nasal hemianopia, homonymous hemianopia, sectoranopia, quadrantanopia, homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing

options.text: Right parietal optic radiation lesion causing left inferior quadrantanopia
options.rationale: Correct because loss of the left inferior quadrant in both eyes is a right homonymous inferior quadrantanopia; the lecture states parietal loop lesions produce contralateral inferior quadrant defects, matching the vignette.

options.text: Midline optic chiasm compression causing left inferior quadrantanopia
options.rationale: Wrong because chiasmal compression produces heteronymous temporal field loss (bitemporal), not a homonymous inferior quadrant deficit in both eyes.

options.text: Right temporal optic radiation lesion causing left inferior quadrantanopia
options.rationale: Wrong because temporal (Meyer's) loop lesions produce superior quadrant defects; a right temporal lesion would cause left superior quadrantanopia, not inferior.

options.text: Left primary visual cortex lesion causing left inferior quadrantanopia
options.rationale: Wrong because a left V1 lesion would affect the right visual field; laterality is inconsistent—right-sided lesion explains left visual field loss.

options.text: Bilateral optic nerve disease causing left inferior quadrantanopia
options.rationale: Wrong because bilateral optic nerve disease causes diffuse or symmetric monocular deficits, not a homonymous quadrant loss in both eyes which localizes to retrochiasmal pathways.

question: A patient with sudden onset of visual symptoms describes flashing colored lights and moving geometric shapes in one visual hemifield. Which lecture cause is most consistent with these transient positive visual phenomena?
cited_learning_objective: Migraine, Visual Auras, etc.

options.text: Occipital lobe seizure producing visual aura
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists occipital seizures as a cause of pulsing colored lights and moving geometric shapes, which are positive visual phenomena localized to inferior visual association cortex or occipital regions.

options.text: Charles Bonnet release hallucination producing formed scenes
options.rationale: Wrong because Charles Bonnet–type release phenomena produce formed complex hallucinations in the setting of visual deprivation, not brief geometric auras typical of occipital seizures.

options.text: Retinal detachment producing moving shapes
options.rationale: Wrong because retinal detachment can cause photopsias but the lecture emphasizes cortical causes like seizures for colored, geometric, hemifield-limited auras; retinal causes are monocular and peripheral.

options.text: Migraine aura from cortical spreading depression
options.rationale: Wrong because while migraine auras can produce similar visual phenomena, the vignette's focal hemifield and seizure-like features point to occipital seizure; migraine aura would be correct if symptoms were stereotyped and migrainous.

options.text: Toxic metabolic delirium causing formed hallucinations
options.rationale: Wrong because toxic metabolic states cause diffuse, often complex hallucinations and altered consciousness, not focal hemifield geometric auras localized to occipital cortex as in seizures.

question: A patient with a lesion affecting layers 1 and 2 of the lateral geniculate nucleus would most likely have impairment in which type of visual information, according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: The lateral geniculate nucleus maintains the precise retinotopy of the ganglion cells that project to it; facilitates the order seen in the primary visual cortex which it projects to.

options.text: Light and motion processing deficits
options.rationale: Correct because layers 1 and 2 of the LGN are magnocellular and carry light and motion information; damage to these layers impairs motion detection as described in the lecture.

options.text: Color and fine spatial discrimination deficits
options.rationale: Wrong because color and fine spatial detail are carried by parvocellular layers (3–6); damage to magnocellular layers would not primarily cause color discrimination loss.

options.text: Monocular central acuity loss
options.rationale: Wrong because monocular central acuity loss is typically retinal or optic nerve in origin; LGN layer lesions produce homonymous field deficits rather than isolated monocular central acuity loss.

options.text: Superior visual field only deficits
options.rationale: Wrong because LGN layers are organized by eye input and function, not strictly by superior versus inferior field; quadrant-specific deficits relate more to radiations and cortical mapping.

options.text: Heteronymous temporal field loss
options.rationale: Wrong because heteronymous temporal field loss arises from chiasmal crossing fiber damage, not selective LGN layer injury which produces contralateral homonymous deficits.

question: A patient with a lesion that interrupts the retinogeniculate projection before the LGN would present with which type of deficit, per the lecture distinctions between prechiasmal and postchiasmal lesions?
cited_learning_objective: Recall, recognize, and apply the meanings of common descriptive terminology: hemianopia, quadrantanopia, homonymous, heteronymous, macular sparing

options.text: Monocular visual field loss or scotoma
options.rationale: Correct because lesions anterior to the optic chiasm (retina or optic nerve) produce monocular deficits such as scotomas or complete vision loss in one eye, as the lecture outlines.

options.text: Contralateral homonymous hemianopia
options.rationale: Wrong because homonymous hemianopia results from postchiasmal lesions (optic tract, LGN, radiations, cortex), not prechiasmal retinogeniculate interruption.

options.text: Bitemporal hemianopia
options.rationale: Wrong because bitemporal loss arises from midline chiasmal lesions damaging crossing nasal fibers, not prechiasmal lesions confined to one eye's pathway.

options.text: Quadrantanopia in both eyes
options.rationale: Wrong because quadrantanopia is a homonymous deficit from geniculocalcarine tract lesions behind the chiasm; prechiasmal lesions cause monocular, not homonymous, quadrant losses.

options.text: Macular sparing central loss
options.rationale: Wrong because macular sparing is a cortical phenomenon seen with V1 lesions and vascular anastomoses, not a feature of prechiasmal retinogeniculate damage.

question: A patient with a lesion in the right optic tract complains of left visual field loss in both eyes. Which lecture statement explains why optic tract lesions produce this pattern?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Optic tract contains fibers for contralateral visual field
options.rationale: Correct because the optic tract carries input from the ipsilateral temporal retina and contralateral nasal retina representing the contralateral visual field, so a right tract lesion causes left homonymous hemianopia as the lecture explains.

options.text: Optic tract contains only ipsilateral eye fibers
options.rationale: Wrong because the optic tract contains combined input from both eyes representing the contralateral visual field, not only ipsilateral eye fibers; this misstatement would be correct only if the tract carried monocular fibers.

options.text: Optic tract lesions produce heteronymous defects
options.rationale: Wrong because heteronymous defects arise at or before the chiasm; optic tract lesions produce homonymous contralateral deficits as described in the lecture.

options.text: Optic tract damage spares contralateral visual field
options.rationale: Wrong because optic tract damage directly affects the contralateral visual field; sparing would require intact tract fibers, not lesion.

options.text: Optic tract lesions cause monocular scotomas
options.rationale: Wrong because monocular scotomas result from retinal or optic nerve lesions anterior to the chiasm; optic tract lesions produce homonymous binocular field loss.

question: A patient with a lesion that interrupts the inferior bank of the calcarine sulcus will most likely lose which portion of the visual field, based on the lecture's dorsal-ventral mapping?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Superior visual field loss contralateral to lesion
options.rationale: Correct because the inferior bank of the calcarine sulcus receives projections for the superior visual field (via Meyer's loop), so damage there causes contralateral superior field deficits as the lecture maps dorsal-ventral relationships.

options.text: Inferior visual field loss ipsilateral to lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because inferior field loss would localize to the superior bank of calcarine cortex and would be contralateral, not ipsilateral; this option reverses both vertical and lateral mapping.

options.text: Central macular loss only
options.rationale: Wrong because macular representation is at the occipital pole and not limited to the inferior bank; isolated macular loss would require occipital pole involvement rather than inferior calcarine damage.

options.text: Bitemporal peripheral loss
options.rationale: Wrong because bitemporal loss arises from chiasmal lesions, not focal calcarine sulcus damage which produces homonymous cortical field deficits.

options.text: Monocular superior quadrant loss
options.rationale: Wrong because monocular quadrant losses are retinal or optic nerve phenomena; cortical inferior bank lesions produce binocular contralateral superior quadrant deficits.

question: A patient with a lesion that interrupts the parvocellular layers of the LGN would most likely have which selective deficit, according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: The lateral geniculate nucleus maintains the precise retinotopy of the ganglion cells that project to it; facilitates the order seen in the primary visual cortex which it projects to.

options.text: Impaired color and fine spatial discrimination
options.rationale: Correct because parvocellular layers (3–6) carry high-resolution spatial and color information; damage to these layers impairs color and fine detail perception as the lecture describes.

options.text: Impaired light and motion detection
options.rationale: Wrong because light and motion are primarily transmitted by magnocellular layers (1–2); parvocellular damage affects color and spatial resolution instead.

options.text: Complete monocular blindness
options.rationale: Wrong because monocular blindness results from retinal or optic nerve lesions anterior to the LGN; selective LGN layer damage produces homonymous field deficits or modality-specific impairments, not complete monocular blindness.

options.text: Heteronymous temporal field loss
options.rationale: Wrong because heteronymous temporal loss is due to chiasmal crossing fiber damage, not selective parvocellular LGN layer injury which affects modality rather than heteronomy.

options.text: Isolated macular sparing
options.rationale: Wrong because macular sparing is a cortical vascular phenomenon; LGN parvocellular damage would not produce the classic cortical macular sparing pattern described in the lecture.

question: A patient with a lesion in the left temporal lobe near the inferior horn of the lateral ventricle reports a right superior quadrant visual loss. Which vascular territory is most likely implicated based on the lecture's discussion of optic radiation blood supply?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Left posterior cerebral artery branch involvement
options.rationale: Correct because the temporal (Meyer's) loop and occipital cortex are primarily supplied by PCA branches; lesions in this territory can produce contralateral superior quadrantanopia as the lecture notes.

options.text: Left middle cerebral artery branch involvement
options.rationale: Wrong because MCA supplies much of the parietal radiations and lateral cortex; while MCA infarcts can affect optic radiations, PCA is the classic supply for temporal loop and occipital cortex causing superior quadrant loss.

options.text: Anterior choroidal artery infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because anterior choroidal supply can affect LGN and internal capsule structures causing variable deficits, but temporal loop superior quadrant loss is more classically linked to PCA territory.

options.text: Internal carotid artery occlusion at bifurcation
options.rationale: Wrong because ICA bifurcation occlusion would produce widespread hemispheric ischemia and not the focal temporal loop superior quadrant deficit specifically associated with PCA branches.

options.text: Superior cerebellar artery infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because superior cerebellar artery supplies cerebellum and brainstem structures, not temporal lobe optic radiations; it cannot explain the described visual quadrant loss.

question: A patient with a lesion that interrupts the geniculocalcarine projection to the superior bank of the calcarine sulcus will most likely lose which part of the visual field, per the lecture mapping?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Inferior visual field contralateral to lesion
options.rationale: Correct because projections to the superior bank of the calcarine sulcus carry inferior visual field information (parietal loop), so interruption there causes contralateral inferior field loss as the lecture describes.

options.text: Superior visual field ipsilateral to lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because superior field loss would map to the inferior bank and deficits are contralateral, not ipsilateral; this option reverses both vertical and lateral mapping.

options.text: Central macular loss only
options.rationale: Wrong because macular representation is at the occipital pole and not limited to the superior bank; isolated macular loss would require occipital pole involvement.

options.text: Bitemporal peripheral loss
options.rationale: Wrong because bitemporal loss arises from chiasmal lesions, not interruption of geniculocalcarine fibers to the superior calcarine bank which produce homonymous inferior field deficits.

options.text: Monocular inferior field loss
options.rationale: Wrong because monocular field losses are prechiasmal; geniculocalcarine interruptions produce binocular homonymous deficits contralateral to the lesion.

question: A patient with a lesion that selectively disrupts magnocellular input to V1 would most likely have difficulty with which visual function, according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: The lateral geniculate nucleus maintains the precise retinotopy of the ganglion cells that project to it; facilitates the order seen in the primary visual cortex which it projects to.

options.text: Motion detection and temporal resolution deficits
options.rationale: Correct because magnocellular pathways (LGN layers 1–2) convey light and motion information and feed dorsal stream processing for temporal and motion detection as described in the lecture.

options.text: Color discrimination and fine detail deficits
options.rationale: Wrong because color and fine spatial detail are parvocellular functions; magnocellular disruption impairs motion and temporal resolution rather than color discrimination.

options.text: Face recognition impairment only
options.rationale: Wrong because face recognition depends on ventral stream parvocellular input to inferior temporal cortex; magnocellular loss would not selectively cause isolated prosopagnosia.

options.text: Heteronymous peripheral field loss
options.rationale: Wrong because heteronymous peripheral loss is due to chiasmal damage, not selective magnocellular pathway disruption which affects modality, not heteronomy.

options.text: Monocular central scotoma
options.rationale: Wrong because monocular central scotomas are retinal or optic nerve phenomena; magnocellular pathway lesions are postretinal and produce binocular modality-specific deficits.

question: A patient with a lesion that interrupts the inferior optic radiations on the left complains of a right superior visual field defect and preserved central vision. Which two lecture concepts together explain the pattern of quadrant loss with central sparing?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the pathway information from the visual fields take through the retina, optic nerve, optic chiasm, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus (including LGN layers), optic radiations (posterior limb of internal capsule included), primary visual cortex, and visual association cortices

options.text: Left temporal (Meyer's) loop lesion with cortical macular sparing
options.rationale: Correct because Meyer's loop lesions produce contralateral superior quadrantanopia and cortical macular sparing can occur when central representation is preserved by collateral arterial supply; combining radiation localization and macular sparing explains the vignette per the lecture.

options.text: Left parietal loop lesion with macular sparing
options.rationale: Wrong because parietal loop lesions produce inferior quadrant deficits, not superior; macular sparing is cortical and would not change the loop-specific quadrant mapping.

options.text: Right temporal loop lesion without macular sparing
options.rationale: Wrong because laterality is reversed and absence of macular sparing contradicts the preserved central vision described; correct localization requires left temporal loop with sparing.

options.text: Left LGN infarct producing superior quadrant loss
options.rationale: Wrong because LGN infarcts produce variable sectoranopias but Meyer's loop temporal radiation lesions are the classic cause of isolated superior quadrantanopia; LGN involvement would not specifically explain macular sparing.

options.text: Optic chiasm compression producing superior quadrant loss
options.rationale: Wrong because chiasmal compression causes heteronymous temporal field loss, not a homonymous superior quadrant deficit with macular sparing.
